1: Zedekiah is installed as king over Coniah — the man who came after the throne-cursed Jehoiachin. The succession through puppet-kingship is the political vacuum that the Righteous Branch oracle (Jeremiah 23:5-6) addresses: the legitimate Davidic line has been broken through exile and curse; the throne is now occupied by a Babylonian vassal. Jesus fills this vacuum: he is the one in whom the legitimate Davidic throne-right is finally and permanently established (Luke 1:32-33), not through political succession but through resurrection and ascension.
2: 'But he, his servants, and the people of the land refused to listen to the words of the LORD.' The persistent not-listening that characterizes Jeremiah's entire ministry reaches its climax under Zedekiah. Jesus encounters the same refusal: 'Though he had performed so many signs before them, they still would not believe in him' (John 12:37). The pattern — YHWH sends his word; the people refuse — culminates in the rejection of the Word made flesh (John 1:11).
3: Zedekiah sends for Jeremiah to 'inquire of the LORD our God.' The king who will not obey the word still wants the prophet's intercession. Jesus is the great intercessor: 'he always lives to intercede for them' (Hebrews 7:25). But his intercession is not magic that overrides covenant accountability — like Jeremiah, he continues to speak truth to those who will not hear it. The intercession is genuine only where there is genuine turning (John 17 shows his intercession for those who do believe).
4: Jeremiah still moving freely — not yet imprisoned. The pre-arrest phase of the prophet's ministry. Jesus's public ministry of free movement — teaching in the temple, healing in the streets — precedes his arrest. Both prophets have a period of freedom followed by imprisonment at the hands of the civil and religious establishment.
5: Pharaoh's army comes out from Egypt, and the Chaldeans temporarily lift the siege. The temporary relief that the unreliable Egyptian alliance provides. Jesus warns his disciples against trusting in political alliances and military strength: 'do not put your trust in princes, in human beings, who cannot save' (Psalms 146:3, cited by Jesus's disciples in Acts 5:38-39 context). The Egyptian army that seems to help will not ultimately deliver.
6: The word of the LORD comes to Jeremiah even in the midst of the political crisis. Jesus's teaching is similarly uninterrupted by political pressure: 'I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns also, because that is why I was sent' (Luke 4:43). The prophetic word continues regardless of political circumstances.
7: 'Say to the king: Pharaoh's army... will return to Egypt its own land.' The message of the Egyptian alliance's unreliability. Jesus's consistent teaching: do not build on sand (Matthew 7:26-27); do not lay up earthly treasures (Matthew 6:19-21); the things that seem like solid help will collapse. The Egyptian army's retreat is the enacted parable of all worldly security.
8: The Chaldeans will return and capture the city and burn it. The certainty of judgment despite the temporary reprieve. Jesus's warnings about Jerusalem are similarly certain despite the long delay: 'the days will come upon you when your enemies will build an embankment against you and encircle you... they will not leave one stone on another' (Luke 19:43-44). The judgment postponed is not the judgment cancelled.
9: 'Do not deceive yourselves by saying: the Chaldeans will certainly leave us, for they will not leave.' Self-deception about one's safety. Jesus warns against the same: 'many will say to me on that day, Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name...? Then I will tell them plainly: I never knew you' (Matthew 7:22-23). The self-deception of assumed safety without genuine covenant relationship is the most dangerous deception.
10: 'Even if you had defeated the entire Chaldean army and only the wounded were left, they would rise and burn this city.' The hyperbolic impossibility — even a catastrophically defeated Babylon would still prevail. The inevitability of the judgment is complete. At the cross, Jesus endures what seems like the defeat of good by evil — and the resurrection is the 'rising of the wounded' in a different sense: the one who is mortally wounded rises, and through his rising all previous calculations are overturned.
11: The Chaldean army withdrawing because of Pharaoh's army — the narrative moment of false hope. False hope is the setting for Jeremiah's arrest: he attempts to go to Benjamin during what looks like a respite, and is seized. Similarly, the disciples' hope at the resurrection rumors becomes real hope only at the actual appearance of the risen Jesus — before that, the 'respite' of the empty tomb generates confusion rather than certainty (John 20:9: 'they still did not understand from Scripture that Jesus had to rise from the dead').
12: Jeremiah sets out toward the territory of Benjamin — likely to Anathoth, his hometown, to receive his share of a property transaction. The mundane purpose of the journey: personal legal business. The innocence of the errand makes the arrest more striking. Jesus's arrest also comes during ordinary activity — praying in a garden with his disciples (Luke 22:39-46). The violent arrest intrudes into the normal.
13: 'When he reached the Benjamin Gate, a sentinel arrested him saying: You are defecting to the Chaldeans!' The false charge. Jesus was arrested on the charge that he claimed to be the king of the Jews (Luke 23:2-3) and that he stirred up the people (Luke 23:5) — similarly false or distorted accusations. The innocent prophet and the innocent Son of God both face trumped-up political charges designed to justify their imprisonment.
14: 'Jeremiah replied: That is not true! I am not defecting to the Chaldeans. But Irijah refused to listen to him.' The denial of the false charge is ignored. Jesus also denies being what his accusers charge: 'my kingdom is not of this world; if it were, my servants would fight' (John 18:36) — the denial of the political-insurrectionist charge. But the authorities proceed regardless. The innocence of the accused does not prevent the judicial process when the conclusion has been predetermined.
15: 'The officials were furious with Jeremiah. They had him beaten and imprisoned in the house of Jonathan the secretary.' Beaten, then imprisoned in a vaulted dungeon. Jesus was beaten (John 19:1-3: flogged, crown of thorns, struck) and imprisoned overnight before crucifixion. The beating of the prophet at the hands of officials is the pattern fulfilled in the Son — both punishments are unjust, both are inflicted by those who should have known better.
16: Jeremiah in the vaulted dungeon cell — the underground pit (beit habor, the house of the pit). The pit is the OT imagery of death (Psalms 28:1; Jeremiah 30:3; Jeremiah 40:2; Jeremiah 88:6: 'I am counted among those who go down to the pit'). Jesus's descent into death is the fulfillment of all pit-imagery: 1 Peter 3:18-20 ('he went and made proclamation to the imprisoned spirits'); Ephesians 4:9 ('he descended to the lower, earthly regions'). The pit of Jeremiah's dungeon is the shadow of the death-pit into which the Son descends.
17: 'King Zedekiah sent for him and questioned him privately: Is there a word from the LORD?' The private consultation — the king who will not obey publicly seeks divine guidance privately. Pilate's private consultation with Jesus (John 18:33-38: 'are you the king of the Jews?' — the private questioning) follows the same pattern. Both rulers privately acknowledge the prophet-king's authority while publicly capitulating to the demands of the crowd.
18: 'What wrong have I done to you, your servants, or this people, that you have thrown me into prison?' The self-defense of the innocent prisoner. Jesus before Annas: 'I have spoken openly to the world... why do you ask me? Question those who heard me' (John 18:20-21). Both Jeremiah and Jesus appeal to the absence of offense — their teaching has been public and consistent; their imprisonment is without just cause.
19: 'And where are your prophets who prophesied: the king of Babylon will not attack you?' The false prophets have been proven wrong; only the true prophet who predicted judgment has been vindicated. Jesus's own predictions come true despite opposition: 'I told you, and you do not believe. The works that I do in my Father's name bear witness about me' (John 10:25). The vindication of the true prophet comes through the fulfillment of what he predicted.
20: Jeremiah pleads with the king not to send him back to the dungeon, where he will die. The prophet's vulnerability — he can be killed by the king's whim. Jesus also was at the mercy of those with earthly authority: 'do you refuse to speak to me? Do you not know that I have authority to release you and authority to crucify you?' (John 19:10). Both Pilate's authority and Zedekiah's authority are real within their limits — but both are ultimately derivative (John 19:11: 'you would have no power over me if it were not given to you from above').
21: Zedekiah gives the order: Jeremiah transferred to the court of the guard, given bread daily until all the bread was gone. The minimal provision — enough to live on, not enough to be comfortable. Jesus provides the bread that sustains more deeply: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never go hungry' (John 6:35). The daily bread given to the imprisoned prophet is a shadow of the bread that the Son gives from his own person.
